title: Integrate PostHog Analytics with Next.js App Router
summary: PostHog analytics integration тАФ provider setup, event tracking, feature flags, user identification, and privacy-compliant configuration for Next.js App Router.
declaration: I will integrate PostHog analytics into this Next.js App Router project. This includes installing the SDK, creating a PostHog provider, setting up automatic pageview tracking, and configuring event capture. I will NOT load PostHog in a way that blocks rendering or skip cookie consent configuration. If you need a different analytics tool (Mixpanel, Amplitude, etc.), say so before I begin.
instructions:
## Integrate PostHog Analytics with Next.js App Router

### 1. Install dependencies
- Run `npm install posthog-js posthog-node`.
- Verify both packages appear in `package.json` dependencies.
- `posthog-js` is the client-side SDK for browser tracking. `posthog-node` is the server-side SDK for server events and feature flags.

### 2. Environment variables
- Check if `.env.local` exists. If not, create it (but NEVER commit it).
- Ensure `.env.local` is listed in `.gitignore`.
- Add the following variables:
  ```
  NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY=phc_...
  NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST=https://us.i.posthog.com
  ```
- Get the project API key from the PostHog dashboard > Project Settings.
- The host depends on your PostHog instance: `https://us.i.posthog.com` (US cloud), `https://eu.i.posthog.com` (EU cloud), or your self-hosted URL.
- Both variables are prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` because the client-side SDK needs them. The project API key is designed to be public тАФ it is NOT a secret.

### 3. Create the PostHog provider
- Create `components/PostHogProvider.tsx` with `'use client'` directive.
- Import `posthog` from `posthog-js` and `PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react`.
- Initialize PostHog inside the component with the env vars.
- Call `posthog.init()` with the API key and config options:
  - `api_host`: set to `NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST`.
  - `person_profiles`: set to `'identified_only'` to only create person profiles for identified users (reduces costs).
  - `capture_pageview`: set to `false` тАФ you will handle pageviews manually to work correctly with Next.js client-side navigation.
  - `capture_pageleave`: set to `true` for session replay accuracy.
- Wrap children with `<PostHogProvider client={posthog}>`.
- Use `typeof window !== 'undefined'` guard before calling `posthog.init()` to prevent SSR initialization.

### 4. Add the provider to the root layout
- Open `app/layout.tsx`.
- Import the PostHog provider component.
- Wrap the `{children}` with the provider, inside the `<body>` tag.
- The provider must be a Client Component, but `layout.tsx` can remain a Server Component тАФ just import and use the Client Component within it.

### 5. Set up pageview tracking
- Create `components/PostHogPageView.tsx` with `'use client'` directive.
- Import `usePathname` and `useSearchParams` from `next/navigation`.
- Import `usePostHog` from `posthog-js/react`.
- In a `useEffect`, call `posthog.capture('$pageview')` whenever pathname or searchParams change.
- Wrap the component in `<Suspense>` when adding it to the layout, because `useSearchParams` requires it in App Router.
- Add this component inside the PostHog provider in the root layout.

### 6. Track custom events
- In any client component, use the `usePostHog` hook: `const posthog = usePostHog()`.
- Capture events: `posthog.capture('button_clicked', { button_name: 'signup', page: '/pricing' })`.
- Event names should be snake_case and descriptive. Properties should be flat objects тАФ avoid deep nesting.
- For server-side events, use `posthog-node`: import `PostHog` from `posthog-node`, instantiate with the API key, and call `client.capture({ distinctId, event, properties })`.
- Always call `client.shutdown()` when done (or in API route cleanup) to flush events.

### 7. Identify users
- After user login, call `posthog.identify(userId, { email, name, ... })` on the client.
- This links anonymous pre-login events to the user profile.
- For server-side identification, use the node client: `client.identify({ distinctId: userId, properties: { email, name } })`.
- Call `posthog.reset()` on logout to create a new anonymous session тАФ NEVER leave a previous user's identity active.

### 8. Set up feature flags
- Create feature flags in the PostHog dashboard > Feature Flags.
- Client-side: use `useFeatureFlagEnabled('flag-name')` hook from `posthog-js/react` or `posthog.isFeatureEnabled('flag-name')`.
- Server-side: use `posthog-node` to evaluate flags: `const isEnabled = await client.isFeatureEnabled('flag-name', userId)`.
- For Next.js middleware feature flags, use `posthog-node` in `middleware.ts` to evaluate flags and rewrite/redirect based on results.
- Feature flags are fetched asynchronously тАФ handle the loading state. Use `useFeatureFlagEnabled` which returns `undefined` while loading, `true`/`false` when resolved.

### 9. Configure privacy and consent
- For GDPR/CCPA compliance, initialize PostHog with `opt_out_capturing_by_default: true`.
- When user consents, call `posthog.opt_in_capturing()`.
- When user declines, call `posthog.opt_out_capturing()`.
- Store the consent choice in a cookie or localStorage and check it on initialization.
- If using session replay, add `session_recording: { maskAllInputs: true }` to mask sensitive form fields by default.
- Add `disable_session_recording: true` if you do not want session replays at all.

### 10. Verify the setup
- Start the dev server: `npm run dev`.
- Open the browser and navigate through pages.
- Open the PostHog dashboard > Activity > Live Events тАФ confirm pageview events are arriving.
- Click a button with custom event tracking тАФ confirm the custom event appears in Live Events.
- Check the Network tab тАФ confirm PostHog requests go to your configured host (not a third-party domain).
- Test user identification: log in, check the PostHog dashboard > People, confirm the user appears with properties.
- Run `npm run build` тАФ confirm no TypeScript errors.

### Common Errors & Fixes
- **No events appearing in PostHog** тАФ Check that `NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY` is correct and the provider is mounted in the root layout. Also check that an ad blocker is not blocking requests to `posthog.com`.
- **Pageviews not tracking on navigation** тАФ You set `capture_pageview: true` (the default) instead of `false`. The default auto-capture only fires on full page loads, not Next.js client-side navigations. Set it to `false` and use the manual pageview component.
- **"PostHog is not initialized" error** тАФ You are calling PostHog methods before `posthog.init()` completes. Use the `usePostHog` hook inside the provider tree, or check `posthog.__loaded` before calling methods.
- **Hydration mismatch** тАФ You are calling `posthog.isFeatureEnabled()` during server render. Feature flags are client-side async тАФ use them only in effects or after mount, or use `posthog-node` for server-side evaluation.
- **Double pageview events** тАФ You have both `capture_pageview: true` AND the manual pageview component. Use only one approach.
- **Events have wrong distinct_id after logout** тАФ You forgot to call `posthog.reset()` on logout. The previous user's ID persists until reset.

### What NOT to Do
- Do NOT use `capture_pageview: true` with Next.js App Router тАФ it only captures full page loads, missing client-side navigations. Use the manual approach.
- Do NOT call `posthog.init()` on the server тАФ it is a browser-only SDK. Guard with `typeof window !== 'undefined'`.
- Do NOT send sensitive data (passwords, credit card numbers, tokens) as event properties тАФ PostHog stores them in plain text.
- Do NOT hardcode the PostHog API key in source code тАФ use environment variables even though the key is public, to support multiple environments (dev/staging/prod).
- Do NOT forget to call `posthog.reset()` on user logout тАФ it causes event attribution to the wrong user.
- Do NOT block rendering on feature flag loading тАФ always handle the undefined/loading state gracefully.
